questions.question: If the angle between two tangents drawn from a point $P$ to a circle of radius $r$ and center $O$ is $60^\circ$, then the length of $OP$ is:

steps: The line segment joining the center to the external point bisects the angle between the tangents.
The triangle formed by the center, the point of tangency, and the external point is a right-angled triangle.

questions.question: A tangent $PQ$ at a point $P$ of a circle of radius $5 \text{ cm}$ meets a line through the center $O$ at a point $Q$ so that $OQ = 13 \text{ cm}$. The length of $PQ$ is:

steps: The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
This forms a right-angled triangle $OPQ$ where $OQ$ is the hypotenuse.
Apply the Pythagorean theorem: $PQ^2 = OQ^2 - OP^2$.

questions.question: The number of tangents that can be drawn from a point lying inside a circle is:

steps: A tangent touches the circle at exactly one point.
If a point is inside the circle, any line passing through it will intersect the circle at two points (a secant).
Therefore, no tangent can be drawn from an interior point.

questions.question: If two tangents inclined at an angle of $80^\circ$ are drawn to a circle of radius $3 \text{ cm}$, then the angle between the two radii at the center is:

steps: The quadrilateral formed by the center, two points of contact, and the external point has angles summing to $360^\circ$.
The angles at the points of contact are $90^\circ$ each.
The angle at the center and the angle between tangents are supplementary.

questions.question: The length of a tangent from a point $A$ at a distance of $10 \text{ cm}$ from the center of the circle is $8 \text{ cm}$. The radius of the circle is:

steps: The radius, tangent, and the line to the external point form a right triangle.
The distance from the center is the hypotenuse ($10$), and the tangent is one leg ($8$).
